caveats: a: No cited source measures the occupation or wage mix within NAICS 621399 firms.
a: Dietitian and acupuncturist tasks do not represent every included modality.
a: The AMA physician survey is a broad adoption proxy and may not represent nonphysician independent practices.
rho: AMA adoption is self-reported and does not verify reliable deployment or labor release.
rho: Physician workflows and resources differ from the nonphysician practitioner mix in this code.
rho: Implementation will vary materially between regulated clinical care and cash-pay wellness practices.
e: The Census definition demonstrates extreme modality heterogeneity but does not measure LMM eligibility.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad provider margin and may misclassify cash-pay, product-heavy, labor-intensive, or owner-compensated practices.
e: Independent legal status does not ensure transferable operations or recurring revenue.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and not restricted to healthcare or the lower-middle market.
s5: The survey measures intended sale or transfer rather than completed qualifying control events.
s5: No cited source measures transaction frequency for NAICS 621399 or its component modalities.
q: No public source measures contract mix or retained automation benefit for this heterogeneous code.
q: Retention may be much higher in differentiated cash-pay practices than in payer-dependent nursing or clinical services.
q: Implementation difficulty and service displacement are excluded here and represented in rho, d5, and o.
d5: Occupation projections are not direct measures of constant-price demand for independent 621399 firms.
d5: Dietitians and nurse midwives represent only part of the code and their shares are unknown.
d5: Discretionary wellness demand, payer coverage, state scope rules, and substitution can differ sharply across modalities.
o: The evidence covers selected occupations rather than the full revenue-weighted 621399 basket.
o: Operator requirement differs substantially between childbirth, respiratory care, dental hygiene, acupuncture, nutrition counseling, and wellness services.
o: Some demand may persist but shift to a different provider type, integrated setting, or software-enabled channel.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Offices of All Other Miscellaneous Health Practitioners

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Practices receive patient cash, commercial insurance, Medicare or Medicaid where covered, employer or institutional contract, per-visit, per-procedure, package, membership, and care-management payments; economics vary sharply between reimbursed licensed care and cash-pay wellness-oriented services.
lens.excluded_activities: Practices separately classified as physicians, dentists, chiropractors, optometrists, mental health specialists, physical or occupational therapists, speech therapists, audiologists, or podiatrists; hospital-owned or other captive departments; product-only retailers; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market independent practices providing recurring or repeat health services to external patients under NAICS 621399, including offices of acupuncturists, dental hygienists, denturists, dietitians, homeopaths, hypnotherapists, inhalation or respiratory therapists, midwives, naturopaths, and registered or licensed practical nurses.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET describes tasks for dietitians and acupuncturists, two included occupations, while the AMA surveys physicians across specialties about AI use cases; none provides a wage-weighted task distribution for NAICS 621399. Transformation: histories, records, care-plan drafts, education materials, research synthesis, patient messaging, scheduling, coding, billing, and portions of routine counseling are mapped to exposed work, while physical assessment, procedures, childbirth care, individualized judgment, and accountable treatment remain less exposed. Direction of error: these example occupations may overstate exposure for highly procedural nursing, midwifery, dental-hygiene, or respiratory work, but may understate it for nutrition, education, intake, and administrative-heavy practices.
caveats: No cited source measures the occupation or wage mix within NAICS 621399 firms.
Dietitian and acupuncturist tasks do not represent every included modality.
The AMA physician survey is a broad adoption proxy and may not represent nonphysician independent practices.
a.change_evidence: A wage-linked time study across the actual 621399 revenue and employment mix, separating each practitioner type, clinical and administrative occupations, counseling, physical treatment, documentation, billing, and already-automated work, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: The code mixes hands-on treatment with information-rich counseling and extensive records. AI has meaningful reach into preparation, documentation, education, and administration, but the broad practitioner mix prevents a high-confidence wage-weighted estimate.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS projects dietitian and nutritionist employment to grow 6 percent and nurse-midwife employment to grow 11 percent from 2024 to 2034, and attributes demand to preventive care, aging, chronic disease, and healthcare-service needs. Transformation: these included but non-exhaustive occupations are converted into a five-year constant-price demand ratio for the current 621399 basket, with a wide range for the unknown modality weights and for reimbursement, birth, chronic-disease, wellness, and substitution trends. Direction of error: the selected occupations may overstate growth if the code is weighted toward slower cash-pay or marginal modalities, but may understate it if advanced nursing and preventive nutrition account for more scaled-firm activity.
caveats: Occupation projections are not direct measures of constant-price demand for independent 621399 firms.
Dietitians and nurse midwives represent only part of the code and their shares are unknown.
Discretionary wellness demand, payer coverage, state scope rules, and substitution can differ sharply across modalities.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price receipts, visits, procedures, active patients, bookings, referral volumes, payer-authorized utilization, and churn for NAICS 621399 segmented by practitioner type and payer model would materially change this range.
d5.rationale: Representative included occupations have positive outlooks, and aging, chronic disease, preventive care, and expanded nonphysician practice support growth. The code's unknown revenue mix and exposure to discretionary cash-pay spending justify a range spanning contraction through strong expansion.

caveats: The Census definition demonstrates extreme modality heterogeneity but does not measure LMM eligibility.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad provider margin and may misclassify cash-pay, product-heavy, labor-intensive, or owner-compensated practices.
Independent legal status does not ensure transferable operations or recurring revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census using business databases, licensing records, payer enrollment, websites, clinician rosters, revenue mix, recurring-patient metrics, owner production, external customer share, and normalized EBITDA by practitioner modality would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The official code covers independent practitioner offices serving external patients, so many scaled firms meet the service lens. Eligibility is reduced by single-practitioner owner dependence, modality-specific licensing and payer constraints, practices dominated by retail products or nonrecurring packages, facility-controlled arrangements, weak clinical management, and the wide gap between reimbursed medical care and cash-pay wellness models.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621399 ($9,673,437,477) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621399 ($19,100,045,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $152,113 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621399); class <5: 24,879 firms, avg revenue/firm $248,362 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $39,241 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,071 firms, avg revenue/firm $831,844 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $131,431 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,436 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,672,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $264,193 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 784 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,640,608 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $575,216 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 160 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,748,212 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,540,218 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $28,639,952 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,525,112 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 284

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET shows that dietitians assess needs, interpret laboratory tests, counsel, personalize plans, and keep histories, while acupuncturists collect histories, diagnose, educate, maintain care records, and perform hands-on treatments; BLS describes nurse midwives performing examinations, prenatal care, delivery, emergency management, and patient education. Transformation: physical assessment, procedures, childbirth, regulated decisions, personalized counseling, and accountable treatment are treated as operator-required, while generic education, plan drafting, monitoring, records, scheduling, and some routine follow-up can be self-served or software-supplied. Direction of error: current task descriptions may overstate human need as remote and AI tools improve, but may understate it where licensure, physical presence, patient trust, adherence coaching, and safety preserve practitioner involvement.
caveats: The evidence covers selected occupations rather than the full revenue-weighted 621399 basket.
Operator requirement differs substantially between childbirth, respiratory care, dental hygiene, acupuncture, nutrition counseling, and wellness services.
Some demand may persist but shift to a different provider type, integrated setting, or software-enabled channel.
o.change_evidence: Buyer and patient studies plus episode-level data by practitioner type showing self-service, software-only completion, channel shift, repeat use, adverse events, payer acceptance, and substitution to other providers would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Most included modalities retain a physical, relational, licensed, or accountable clinical element, but nutrition education and other information-heavy services face meaningful self-service and software substitution. The mixed code requires a wider range than a single clinical specialty.

caveats: No public source measures contract mix or retained automation benefit for this heterogeneous code.
Retention may be much higher in differentiated cash-pay practices than in payer-dependent nursing or clinical services.
Implementation difficulty and service displacement are excluded here and represented in rho, d5, and o.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level diligence segmented by practitioner type on cash versus payer revenue, billing units, fee schedules, capitation, packages and memberships, renewal cadence, technology costs, staffing changes, utilization, price changes, and realized gross margin would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Cash-pay, package, and membership practices may keep a substantial portion of administrative productivity until competition reprices services, while coded reimbursement can also leave short-run savings with the operator. Retention falls where payers set rates, institutional contracts demand sharing, low barriers intensify price competition, software fees absorb savings, or saved clinician time becomes added capacity rather than removable cost.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: the AMA's 2026 U.S. physician survey measures self-reported AI incorporation or awareness and identifies documentation, summarization, privacy, safety, training, and liability considerations. Transformation: adoption momentum is applied to exposed 621399 work over five years, discounted for small-practice software fragmentation, variable EHR use, protected health information, clinical validation, state scope rules, cash-pay workflow differences, staff training, and licensed review. Direction of error: physician results may overstate implementation resources available to small miscellaneous practitioners, while lightweight consumer, scheduling, documentation, and practice-management tools may spread faster in cash-pay practices than in physician organizations.
caveats: AMA adoption is self-reported and does not verify reliable deployment or labor release.
Physician workflows and resources differ from the nonphysician practitioner mix in this code.
Implementation will vary materially between regulated clinical care and cash-pay wellness practices.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor telemetry and practice surveys segmented by 621399 practitioner type, practice size, payer model, EHR use, deployed use case, error and override rate, staff time saved, and realized headcount or capacity effects would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Documentation, education, intake, communication, and office workflows can adopt assistive tools incrementally. The diversity of practice systems and regulation, plus safety and privacy requirements, makes uniform implementation unlikely.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Transformation: this intention rate is discounted for plans that do not close, internal or family transfers without qualifying control, and practitioner firms whose licenses, personal reputation, patient relationships, payer status, or owner production cannot transfer. Direction of error: the broad employer sample may overstate completed qualifying events, but an aging owner base and consolidation of scaled clinical practices could make some modalities more transaction-prone.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and not restricted to healthcare or the lower-middle market.
The survey measures intended sale or transfer rather than completed qualifying control events.
No cited source measures transaction frequency for NAICS 621399 or its component modalities.
s5.change_evidence: Completed control-deal counts and eligible-firm denominators by included practitioner type from PitchBook, Irving Levin Associates, Axial, healthcare brokers, lender records, licensing boards, payer-change-of-ownership filings, and management interviews would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A multi-practitioner platform with institutionalized referrals, records, billing, and management can transfer, but many miscellaneous health practices are inseparable from a licensed or personally branded owner. Intentions therefore provide only a loose upper anchor.

principal_driver: Information-heavy counseling and administration coexist with repeat patient relationships across a potentially broad pool of scaled practitioner firms.
principal_weakness: The code's extreme modality heterogeneity makes average automation, demand, retention, and transfer assumptions fragile without firm-level mix data.
